integrity.statement: Every figure is copied from a committed, reviewed per-kernel result under bench/results/; no number is authored in prose. Local raw benchmark output stays under the gitignored bench/results/local/ scratch directory.
integrity.isolation_disclaimer: All comparison runs are non-isolated (taskset affinity on a shared workstation, no kernel isolcpus reservation). Absolute timings are noisy; only the fastest-first ranking, which holds across orders of magnitude, is promoted. Julia and Go command-line paths and the SystemVerilog cosimulation fixture are process-startup or subprocess dominated and are not runtime performance claims.

notes: MIF-007 measures the Python no-backpressure reference, Python cycle-level valid/ready reference, and a Verilated SystemVerilog fixture over the same 4 096-sample alternating-polarity stream.
The SystemVerilog measurement invokes the Verilated binary as a subprocess and is local regression/cosimulation evidence only, not an FPGA timing or dispatch claim.

provenance.isolation_method: taskset affinity to logical cores 8-11; no kernel isolcpus reservation
provenance.hardware_summary: x86_64, 12 logical CPUs, 6 cores, 2 threads per core

notes: MIF-006 rate decoding is measured for Python and Rust runtime surfaces plus a Julia polyglot-parity reference over the committed 256-event AER fixture; production stays on the Rust runtime path.

Event timestamps, explicit window starts, and decode-window lengths are bounded to the shared u64 nanosecond domain on the Python and Rust/PyO3 surfaces; u64 stop overflow fails closed. The Julia parity reference uses Int64 timestamps and reproduces the rate-decode mathematics (channel 0 = 8 / 4096).
Julia is measured through the package CLI harness; the recorded timing includes Julia process startup, so it ranks last and is a comparison reference, not a runtime path.
Non-isolated local comparison run under taskset affinity and heavy host load; do not treat as a production performance claim.

notes: Local non-isolated comparison benchmark only; not a production latency claim.
MIF-018 PCIe DMA ring mock has allocated Python, Rust, and Go comparison surfaces; the Python and Rust runtime paths replay 256 frames through the ring buffer.

Go is a polyglot-parity reference measured via CLI subprocess (go run); the Go scaffold has no ring buffer, so it performs 256 sequential frame encode/decode round-trips over the same frame codec and validates the summed sequence checksum (32640).
This report reflects fail-closed reserved-header, sequence-order, and timestamp-monotonic replay semantics.

notes: Causal streaming merge-trigger: per-sample push on a persistent engine (push_single) and a 512-sample session including construction (session_512); Python and Rust/PyO3 runtime surfaces.

The Python-visible Rust figures include per-call FFI conversion overhead; the in-process Rust per-push cost, measured by the criterion bench scpn-mif-rs/crates/mif-kinematic/benches/streaming_trigger.rs on the same host and affinity, was mean 43.3 ns / median 29.2 ns (non-isolated, n=2 channels).

Non-isolated local comparison run under taskset affinity and heavy host load (load average ~34-38); absolute timings are noisy and means are outlier-inflated. The fastest-first ranking (rust < python) holds across the distribution; do not treat as a production performance claim.
max_ns is recorded per surface because the streaming engine's claim class is worst-case per-sample cost, not throughput; under this host load the max reflects scheduler preemption, not the kernel.

provenance.isolation_method: taskset affinity to logical cores 8-11; no kernel isolcpus reservation
provenance.hardware_summary: x86_64, 12 logical CPUs, 6 cores, 2 threads per core

notes: Probabilistic trigger propagation (P(lock)/P(violation) with the FIRE/ABORT_UNSAFE/HOLD operating point): a 64-sample approach trace (the per-shot planning call) and a 4096-sample campaign trace; Python and Rust/PyO3 runtime surfaces.

The FFI boundary is zero-copy in both directions (read-only NumPy views in, per-sample probability columns out as NumPy arrays); the per-call "batch" conversion pattern was measured first and lost the 4096-sample group to object-conversion overhead (134 ms vs 70 ms median) before the boundary was flipped to columns.

Both backends assemble the same per-sample dataclass trace on the Python side, so the 4096-sample ratio (~1.4x) is bounded by shared object construction, not the kernel; the 64-sample per-shot call shows the kernel ratio (~2.3x).

Non-isolated local comparison run under taskset affinity and heavy host load (load average ~50-61); absolute timings are noisy and means are outlier-inflated. The fastest-first ranking (rust < python) holds in both groups across the distribution; do not treat as a production performance claim.
max_ns is recorded per surface; under this host load the max reflects scheduler preemption, not the kernel.
